cards.text: A {{c1::copy constructor}} is a special constructor used to create a new object as a {{c2::copy}} of an existing object of the same type.

cards.question: Define a copy constructor for this class:
cards.code:
class Fraction
{
private:
    int m_num;
    int m_den;
public:
    Fraction(int num, int den)
        : m_num{num}, m_den{den} { }
    // Add copy constructor
};
cards.explanation: Copy constructor takes a const reference to another Fraction and copies its members.

cards.front: When is the copy constructor called?
cards.back:
• When initializing a new object from an existing object
  • When passing an object by value to a function
  • When returning an object by value from a function (unless elided)

cards.text: If you don't provide a copy constructor, the compiler generates an {{c1::implicit copy constructor}} that performs a {{c2::memberwise copy}} (shallow copy) of all members.

cards.question: How many times is the copy constructor called?
cards.code:
Fraction f1{3, 4};
Fraction f2{f1}; // 1
Fraction f3 = f1; // 2
printFraction(f1); // 3 (pass by value)
Fraction f4 = getFraction(); // 4 (return by value)
cards.answer: Potentially 4 times, but copy elision may eliminate some of these copies (especially 4).
cards.explanation: Copy constructor is called when copying objects. Modern compilers often elide copies when possible.

cards.front: Why must the copy constructor parameter be a const reference?
cards.back: If it took the parameter by value, calling the copy constructor would require making a copy, which would call the copy constructor again, creating infinite recursion. Reference is required; const is best practice.

cards.concept: When you need to write a custom copy constructor
cards.explanation:
Write a custom copy constructor when:
  • Class manages dynamic memory (need deep copy, not shallow)
  • Class manages other resources (file handles, network connections)
  • Default memberwise copy would be incorrect or insufficient
  • Need to perform additional actions during copying
For simple classes with only fundamental types or members that copy properly, the implicit copy constructor is fine.
examples: // Need custom: class String { char* m_data; };  // dynamic memory
// Implicit OK: class Point { int x, y; };  // just ints

cards.back: A memberwise copy that copies pointer values directly, resulting in two pointers pointing to the same memory
A copy that allocates new memory and copies the pointed-to data, resulting in two independent copies

cards.text: Best practice: Pass class objects by {{c1::const reference}} instead of by value to {{c2::avoid unnecessary copies}} and improve performance.

cards.front: How can you prevent a class from being copied?
cards.back:
Delete the copy constructor: ClassName(const ClassName&) = delete;
This causes a compile error if anyone tries to copy an object of that class.
